data.title: Hertz: Another Chance To Sell
data.description:
Over the last couple of years, one of the worst performing stocks has been Hertz (NASDAQ:HTZ). The rental car giant has seen its shares plunge as a badly timed investment in electric vehicles has led to large losses and mounting debt. Recently, shares have rallied quite a bit from their 52-week low, but this remains a name that's in a very tough spot financially.

Previous coverage of the name:

It was nearly a year ago when I last took a look at this company, continuing to believe that it was in a tough spot. Hertz was looking to dump a lot of electric vehicles, which resulted in huge depreciation expenses and lower than expected residual sales values. The rise in US interest rates was also troubling for a company that relied significantly on debt to finance its fleet.

Since that article in March 2024, Hertz shares have lost more than a third of their value. That's a tremendous drop when you consider the S&P 500 is up about 18% over the same time. The plunge could have been even worse, but shares have rallied from their multi-year low of $2.47 to over $4.50 a share at one point on Tuesday. Overall, since I went to a sell rating on the name back in December 2022, shares have lost about 73% of their value.

Last week's report:

A little bit before the long holiday weekend, the company reported its fourth quarter results. The headline numbers were not good, as revenues of $2.04 billion were down 7% year over year and missed street estimates by $90 million. The company reported a non-GAAP loss of $1.18, missing the average estimate by almost 50 cents a share. This was the third straight quarter where the company missed estimates on both the top and bottom lines, and the sixth straight bottom line miss.

The Q4 2024 revenue decline came from two main factors. First, average rentable vehicle was down about 6% over the prior year period, mostly due to the company's de-fleeting of 30,000 electric vehicles. Second, revenues per day were down more than 2%. Currently, street analysts see limited growth in the top line this year, but not enough to get back to 2023 levels. Hertz will be looking to generate more revenues per day from its vehicles, while operating a smaller fleet than it previously had.

The problem for Hertz is that expenses are too great currently. Despite the revenue decrease, direct vehicle operating expenses rose over the prior year period. Even though vehicle depreciation improved a bit, the company also saw interest expenses shoot up thanks to the growing debt pile. As a result, the $479 million GAAP loss in the period was up significantly from the $348 million reported a year earlier.

Balance sheet is not in good shape:

Hertz finished 2024 with a little less than $600 million in non-restricted cash on the balance sheet, down about $175 million for the year. However, total debt rose by more than $640 million to over $16.3 billion. The company produced operating cash flow of over $2.2 billion in 2024, down $250 million from a year earlier. Unfortunately, it also spent more than $2.8 billion more on vehicles in 2024 over total sales proceeds. As a reminder, Hertz over the long term is buying new vehicles but then selling used ones at lower prices, so unless it continues to take the fleet down in large numbers, there's not a ton of room for major free cash flow here.

With the rise in interest rates in recent years, Hertz is in a tough spot. As the 10-K filing shows on page 113, over $9 billion of the company's debt comes due by the end of next year, with almost all of the rest due by the end of 2029. A number of these borrowings, including vehicle-related ones, came earlier in this decade and featured very low rates. Last year, interest expenses were about 10.6% of revenues, up from less than 8.5% the year prior, and all of these re-financings that are needed won't help that scenario.

The evolving rental car industry:

Hertz finds itself in a very interesting place currently. Travelers and some business customers still have a need for vehicle rentals, even if only for a short time period. The pandemic obviously crushed demand, not only from reduced consumer travels, but the increase in working from home. Hertz found out the hard way that electric vehicles were not in demand from many renters, and those users unfamiliar with the new technology led to increased repair costs and higher than expected depreciation.

In 2025, however, the biggest threat may not be purely an economic one. Electric vehicle giant Tesla (TSLA) is set to launch its robo-taxi business in June in Austin, Texas, with multiple other cities later in the year and rolling out even more over time. Other competitors like Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo are already providing hundreds of thousands of paid rides per month. As more options like ride-sharing become available, potentially pushing prices of those services way down, the need for vehicle rentals may take a hit.

A look at the current valuation:

With Hertz losing money currently and expected to post only a very small profit next year, the stock looks expensive on a price to earnings basis. Given Monday's rally, shares go for about 48 times next year's currently expected earnings, a tremendous premium to the less than 7 times that competitor Avis Budget Group (CAR) goes for. Avis is in a much better place financially, producing a profit last year (after restructuring costs), while not burning as much cash as Hertz did.

On the analyst side of things, the bears are certainly in charge. The current average price target is $3.39, implying about 25% downside from Tuesday's high. While being very positive on Hertz over time, it seems that the street has finally realized how bad the situation is here. A year ago, the average price target was nearly $10 a share, and less than three years ago it was pushing $30 at its peak.

Final thoughts and recommendation:

After reporting downbeat results for Q4 last week, Hertz continues to be in a tough spot financially. The company's decision to reduce its fleet size led to a drop in revenues, but costs are not coming down at a similar pace. With a bad bet on electric vehicles still working its way through the system, cash burn has certainly continued, increasing overall debt levels at a time where Hertz has a mountain of borrowings to refinance.

With a troubled balance sheet and an uncertain industry moving forward, I'm going to keep a sell rating on this stock. It will take a lot of effort to get this company back to positive overall cash flow, and that might result in a further hit to revenues. The company will have some tough re-financings ahead of it, and it really could use to raise a large amount of money through equity, but it has a less than $1.5 billion market cap. With a high valuation currently, it would not surprise me if recent gains don't last.

data.title: Silicon Valley Conquers The Pentagon As Defense Tech Explodes
data.description:
For a few months now, I’ve been referring to today’s heightened geopolitical climate as the “new red Cold War,” where artificial intelligence [AI]—not necessarily fighter jets and nuclear weapons—serves as the primary battleground between the U.S. and its adversaries, most notably Russia and China.

The numbers tell a compelling story. According to one research firm, the global AI market in aerospace and defense is projected to surge from approximately $28 billion today to a staggering $65 billion by 2034. That’s a solid 9.91% compound annual growth rate [CAGR]. North America alone represents $10.43 billion of this market, and it’s growing even faster at 10.02% annually.

Palantir’s Meteoric Rise

We’re seeing a dramatic reshuffling of the traditional defense sector hierarchy.

Take Palantir Technologies (PLTR). The AI-focused company—founded in 2003 by Peter Thiel, among others, and named for a magical artifact from Lord of the Rings—has seen its stock soar approximately 61% so far this year, after returning a massive 340% in 2024.

Meanwhile, traditional defense giants like Lockheed Martin (LMT), Northrop Grumman (NOC) and General Dynamics (GD) have been struggling, with their combined market value now lower than that of Palantir, whose chief technology officer, Shyam Sankar, recently called the competition between the U.S. and China an “AI arms race.”

And let’s not discount the influence of Elon Musk’s cost-cutting Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) initiative. We could see a push to modernize military procurement, prioritizing software, drones, and robots over traditional hardware. DOGE has already sent tremors through the defense sector, with traditional contractor stocks taking hits as new players like SpaceX, OpenAI and Anduril Industries gain ground.

Vice President Vance Pushes for AI in Paris

This shift isn’t happening in a vacuum, of course. The Trump administration, with Vice President JD Vance at the forefront, is actively pushing for AI development while taking a notably different approach from our European allies.

At the AI Action Summit in Paris last week, Vance made it clear that the U.S. won’t let excessive regulation stifle innovation in this critical sector. “We need our European friends in particular to look to this new frontier with optimism rather than trepidation,” Vance told the audience, which included world leaders, CEOs and scientists from over 100 countries.

This pro-growth stance has already benefited U.S. chipmakers like Intel, whose shares ended last week up more than 23% on news of increased support for domestic chip production. The company has its work cut out for it: Today, some 90% of the world’s most advanced chips are made by the Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC).

Even Google, once hesitant about military contracts, has reversed its stance on AI weapons development. This shift signals a broader change in Silicon Valley’s relationship with defense contracts, opening up new investment opportunities in tech companies that previously stayed away from the sector.

Europe’s Defense Tech Boom Is Just Getting Started

The transformation isn’t limited to the U.S. Facing its own security challenges with Russia, Europe is seeing unprecedented growth in defense tech investment. Venture capital [VC] funding in defense technology hit a record $5.2 billion in 2024, according to a new report by Dealroom data. This marks an incredible fivefold jump from six years ago, making defense one of the fastest growing VC sectors in Europe right now.

For investors, this creates what I see as a once-in-a-generation opportunity. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s target of 3% of GDP for defense spending—roughly $1 trillion annually—suggests sustained government investment in the sector, despite Musk’s promise to cut costs. The key question isn’t whether there will be spending, but rather which companies will capture it.

Positioning for the Biggest Defense Tech Shift in Decades

So where should investors be looking? I see three key areas:

First, companies at the intersection of AI and defense, like Palantir, that are already proving their worth in military applications. These firms are positioning themselves as essential partners in modern warfare capabilities.

Second, domestic semiconductor manufacturers like Intel (INTC) that are critical to both AI development and national security. The Trump administration’s focus on U.S. production could provide significant tailwinds for these companies.

And third, emerging defense tech companies that are disrupting traditional military procurement. While many are not publicly traded yet—Anduril being one such example—keeping an eye on this space could reveal early opportunities as they come to market.

We’re witnessing the biggest transformation in defense technology since the advent of nuclear weapons, and I believe that those who position themselves in this new “AI arms race” could see substantial returns as this multi-decade trend unfolds.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 02/20/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Intapp: Transitioning To Positive Margins On Scale Gains
data.description:
Introduction

I had never heard of Intapp (NASDAQ:INTA) until it appeared in the SA Quant top 10 for 2025, which I analyzed as if it were an ETF in mid-January. The company had the second highest Factor Grades, and thus, after taking a minor position (along with all the top 10 names), I decided to conduct a more thorough analysis of INTA and found a niche software company in the midst of transitioning into positive margins on scale gains. One key question is whether AI will be an opportunity or a threat.

What is Intapp

Intapp, or intelligence applied, is a vertical software company focused on the investment/private equity, legal, accounting, consulting, and real estate sectors, all of which are human capital-intensive. Intapp provides these companies with sales, process organization, and compliance productivity tools. It sells its software as a service (SaaS) and has moved the delivery system and access to the cloud. One key development in its success has been the partnership with Microsoft (MSFT), where Intapp products are deployed on Microsoft Cloud (Azure) and available on the Azure Marketplace and co-sell/marketing. At present, Intapp has over 2,650 customers and 135+ partners that provide data, technology, and services.

Profitability Infection

While the company went public in 2021, the data pre-IPO from 2019 highlights the path to profitability as illustrated in the two charts below. INTA has grown revenue at a 20% rate while its cash cost growth has been choppier with drops and spikes during the pandemic years and has dropped to a 13% pace since mid-2022. The scale gains occurred as the cost of new revenue was marginal or fixed and results in driving EBITDA and cash earnings margins into positive territory for the first time.

According to consensus estimates, sales should continue to grow in the 15% range and drive EBITDA margin to 22% by 2027 (June 2027). This margin gain should drive cash earnings (GAAP net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation) growth by over 60% in 2026-2027, which in turn should generate over US$140m in FCF, which adds a higher level of comfort to the business.

Valuation

INTA is not a cheap stock, which is typical for developing companies moving from operating losses to gains. The market generally continues to value this growth potential at high multiples. The consensus price target of US$77 backs into a P/CE (cash earnings) multiple of 91x or a relative PEG ratio of 1.8, which many investors may find unappealing.

I believe it is better to value the stock by looking out to fiscal 2026 (June 2026). On a 1x PEG, my price target is US$98, which provides a good upside. During 2025, the company should continue to de-risk, posting positive margins that may see upside revisions given the less-than-buyout consensus revenue growth forecasted.

Consensus Peer Comps

As one may imagine, the vertical SaaS sector is full of small and medium sized companies. Few are household names, and the smaller the market cap, the more likely it is to see negative margins, i.e., still scaling. INTA is in line with sector revenue growth expectations, with margins below larger peers and a high earnings growth rate as margins expand. The valuation on an absolute P/CE basis is also in line with the sector, while the relative or PEG ratio seems reasonable for a company transitioning to sustained profitability. The larger peers have garnered substantially higher PEG multiples, which suggests INTA may have room to run as it delivers results.

Quant Grades

I agree with all of the quant grades. I see high growth that can lead to revisions and momentum. The profitability grade should improve on higher growth/scale, as discussed earlier. The valuation is a tough spot, which is why investors should look at INTA on an 18-month basis to fiscal YE26 (June 2026) for estimated growth to impact the share price.

Risk

INTA is a small company in a crowded sector that can face considerable competitive pressure. While the advent of AI is considered a growth driver, its rapid development could make INTA software obsolete or reduce barriers to entry. If the company sees a reversal in growth or margins, the stock price will suffer harshly.

Conclusion

I rate INTA a Buy. The company is delivering value to its customers, further enhanced by the Microsoft Azure partnership, which should allow it to continue growing margins and cash earnings at a 50% rate. Valuation is not absurd on a relative PEG basis but requires an 18-month view for above-market price potential to materialize, barring surprise revenue growth during 2025.

data.title: Lands' End: Attractively Priced, But Tariff Developments Don't Bode Well
data.description:
Decent Annual Returns, But Lost Its Mojo In Recent Months

The stock of Lands' End, Inc. (NASDAQ:LE), a prominent US-based digital retailer (90% of their business is carried out online) of products such as apparel, swimwear, accessories, uniforms, footwear, etc., has done reasonably well over the past year. While it has underperformed other consumer discretionary stocks by around 300bps, it has still managed healthy enough returns of ~22%, and outperformed its Russell 2000 peers by over 800bps.

Having said that, those who pursued LE during the final quarter of last year won’t be best pleased, as it has given up quite a lot of gains (heading into Q4-24, LE was at one stage up by roughly 80%).

While we do appreciate some facets of Lands' End, there are also some risks that could continue to weigh adversely on the share.

Tariff Woes

One of the major reasons why the LE stock has been on a slump off late is because there appears to be a lot of uncertainty over how effectively it will cope in an era of heightened tariffs. An overwhelming majority of LE’s inventory is sourced from 20 countries outside the US, with 5 countries alone accounting for 70% of total merchandise purchases. While management was keen to reiterate their declining exposure to China (currently at less than 6%) and a gradual shift to the Western Hemisphere, there will likely be some repercussions in the near-term (a study by the United States Fashion Industry Association shows that the impact of a tariff shock on apparel retail will most keenly be felt after two months), particularly as LE has so far been following a strategy of not competing on price, and pursuing limited promotions.

This strategy has proven to be very fruitful in helping boost LE’s gross margins to over the 50% mark (the highest since it went public in 2014), and note that on a YTD basis margins have been making consistent progress over the past couple of years.

Going forward, if LE is to keep improving its gross margin position, it will need to pass on these tariffs to the end customer, which may then end up hampering volumes. Already one can see that the topline hasn’t really been growing since 2023 (although a major reason for this could also be the company’s recent impetus on transitioning certain product categories such as footwear and kidswear to the licensing mode of revenue), so passing on higher tariff costs may not necessarily be the most optimal course of action.

Investors shouldn’t play down the impact of this, as so far, even though LE has been facing topline pressure, they have been managing their COGS base even more effectively (COGS is currently declining at 4.5x the pace of revenue). The additional gross profit dollars have enabled them to ramp up SG&A (currently growing at 4% and accounting for 44% of Q3 sales, 250bps higher YoY) investments and include a greater focus on digital marketing initiatives that have helped boost their new customer count. As of Q3, higher marketing was instrumental in driving a 20% increase in new customer count, while on a YTD basis it has been growing at a level of mid-teens.

Inventory Management and FCF Landscape Look Healthy

While LE’s cost base could face some troubles going forward, the company still deserves credit for what a tight ship it runs when it comes to the inventory position. Cash tied up with inventory has been on a downward slump for 3 years now, while LE is also increasingly focusing on inventorying newer and fresher products (which are less prone to write-downs and better positioned to generate full-price sales).

Note that the inventory turnover ratio of LE which has traditionally averaged around 3.6x, is now at its highest point in 5 years at 4.3x. We would expect this to continue to improve when LE publishes its Q4 results next month, as the final quarter is when they typically liquidate the most of their inventory and witness the highest business momentum (for context, over the last 3 years, sales in Q4 alone have accounted for 34% of the annual sales).

Besides a sturdy inventory position, LE’s CAPEX is also expected to be relatively flat at $35m for the second year running, which should help lift the free cash flow position. Even before the Q4 results, it is worth noting that at the current market cap, the LE stock already yields a very impressive FCF yield of over 14%, almost 1000bps better than its long-term average of just 5%.

EV/EBITDA Valuations Look Alluring, In Light Of The Forward EBITDA Growth

The correction in the share price has also made LE look quite attractive from a forward EV/EBITDA basis. For context, this is a business that has traditionally been priced at a multiple of 8.3x (the 5-year average) but can currently be picked up at a multiple of just 6.3x, which implies an approximate discount of almost 25%.

For such a low EV/EBITDA multiple, LE also offers a decent degree of EBITDA growth. After generating EBITDA of $84.3m last year, consensus expects LE to hit EBITDA of over $100m by the end of Feb 2026 (LE follows a Feb fiscal), which would represent CAGR growth of roughly 9%. Getting 9% EBITDA growth for a business priced at 6.3x sounds like a good deal to us.

Closing Thoughts: Suitable Mean-Reversion Candidate, But Standalone Charts Reiterate Ongoing Bearish Pressure

Investors on the lookout for suitable mean-reversion candidates within the broad consumer discretionary universe may take a fancy toward the LE stock, as its current relative strength ratio is around 58% lower than its 5-year average.

However, on the daily charts of LE alone, it’s pretty evident that the bears still have the upper hand, as the stock is now trading well below all its 3 key moving averages (50, 100 and 200) with the percentage differential working out to double-digit levels in all 3 cases.

One can get a broader picture of things by looking at the weekly chart, where we saw the stock break down from an 8-month trend line in early December and has since continued to witness a series of lower lows and lower highs. Besides that, the RSI indicator in the lower end of the chart has not yet hit oversold territory (although it doesn’t necessarily have to either).

Having said that, those who have an aggressive risk appetite may be interested in taking a punt, as we can see that for the last 3 years, LE has chopped around within the $6 to $18 levels, and at the current sub-$12 levels, the risk-reward, which didn’t look so attractive in October, is now looking quite fair.

data.title: M-tron Industries: Stock Looks Overpriced, And A Correction Is Likely Due
data.description:
M-Tron Industries Inc.'s (NYSE:MPTI) stock has been extremely volatile over the last year, swinging from lows of ~$23 to as high as ~$70, a 300% swing within 7 months. Now, it's at ~$44 per share, and I think that a correction is here, albeit a bit early. These price movements suggest a fairly speculative bubble, i.e., the current market pricing is too optimistic. For context, a company that's been pretty stable on all fronts for a few years now and only grew revenue by 16% YoY has little business having a valuation that looks far removed from its underlying cash flow potential and historical performance. My DCF model indicates that buying the stock at its current price has a -100% upside (so it's likely worth half its current price), and putting money in now won't be worth it.

Company Overview

M-Tron Industries specializes in designing and manufacturing precision radio frequency (RF) components and solutions and has over 800 customers and >9500 unique products. The company's core business is organized into three main segments: Filters, Oscillators, and Resonators & Solutions, and its product portfolio includes highly-engineered components such as oscillators and integrated microwave assemblies (IMAs), which are critical in applications like communication systems, radar, electronic warfare, and avionics. If it wasn't clear yet, MPTI is a pretty important player in sectors such as aerospace, defense, and space, with clients like Boeing (BA), Northrop Grumman (NOC), and Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF), among others.

I like the company's positioning for forward growth (it's already signed two new defense contracts in January and February), and I'd say its main growth drivers are increased demand for armament and defense spending, strong R&D investments, and expansion into rapidly growing markets like space and commercial aerospace. Don't just take it from me, though: that's what the company's investor presentation says.

The company also operates in a very large total addressable market (TAM) across industries where analysts expect major growth. For example, defense electronics market (~6% CAGR), satellite communications (SATCOM) (~10% CAGR), and commercial aviation (~7% CAGR) all have pretty solid growth prospects. Given the momentum in program business and new system development, MPTI has a lot to benefit from expanding defense budgets, which is a consequence of increasing global conflicts and modernization programs. Additionally, the company's ongoing R&D and investment in manufacturing processes put it in a position to capture even more high-value clients in its key markets.

It's a Numbers Game

Now, let me break down the key financial metrics that reinforce my thesis for MPTI. To be clear, I'm recommending a Hold for a price correction for investors who want to buy into the company. Into the weeds, then. Over the last three quarters of the current fiscal year, FY2024, MPTI's quarterly revenues have grown modestly-from about $11.2M in Q1 to roughly $13.2M in Q3. This steady, organic growth indicates that while there's a healthy demand for its precision RF components, it isn't spectacular or transformative. Keep that in mind. Also, operating income has followed a similar, incremental trend, rising from around $1.79M in Q1 to nearly $2.92M in Q3. If we look at the gross margins, though, those have remained in the 40-45% range, with slight improvements YoY, but that is a sign that its cost structure is tight in a competitive, capital-intensive market. So, it'd be fair to say that this metric is critical because it shows that the company isn't generating the robust cash flows that would justify the stock's high valuation multiples.

Moreover, the stock's volatility over the past 12 months is a bit of a red flag, in my opinion. The stock went from about $23.90 in early May 2024 to nearly $70 in early December, only to drop back to ~$50 over the last few days and tanking even more as we speak. Sure, spikes and swings happen-it is the stock market, after all. But such dramatic movement suggests that it is market sentiment, rather than solid cash flows, that is driving the price.

When I look at the balance sheet, the picture remains consistent. Although current assets and free cash flow are positive, the modest profit margins and the inherent cost structure in manufacturing high-precision RF components imply that MPTI isn't that big of a growth engine to justify the current high stock price.

I believe that the combination of steady revenue growth, slight margin expansion, and extreme price volatility paints a clear picture: the market's exuberance has driven the stock to unsustainable levels. In essence, the numbers tell a story of a fundamentally sound company that is being overhyped, setting the stage for a likely correction that aligns the market price with the underlying cash flow potential.

The major thing, though, is what the underlying numbers say. I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model that points to a fair value of roughly $23 per share. That's a difference of ~100% from the current price, which is pretty high, and it tells me that at a market price of around $50 (the price on Monday, February 17, 2025), investors are paying nearly double what the fundamentals support. Now, let's look at the valuation.

What Does the Valuation Tell Us?

For my valuation analysis, I assumed an annual revenue of $47M (expected revenue for the current fiscal year; the company looks on track to hit that target). I also kept the cost of revenues at 50%, operating expenses at 25%, CAPEX at 5%, net working capital at 35%, and D&A at 4% of revenue.

Then, I tested different revenue growth scenarios-15%, 16%, and 20%-but the free cash flow I calculated came out to only about $8.2M. I also adjusted the current stock price as it changed in real-time, and the picture seems pretty clearcut. At stock prices between $45 and $50 per share, my DCF model shows a 100% downside, suggesting that the stock is nearly twice as expensive as its fair value.

What this tells me is that even if the company hits more optimistic growth rates (20% is optimistic), its cash generation is too modest to justify the current high valuation. In simple terms, the numbers don't add up-the company's earnings and cash flow are too low right now to support a market price of $50 or higher. Investors seem to be paying a huge premium based more on speculative hype than on solid cash flows. This clear disconnect between the fundamentals and the market price is a key reason why I believe the stock is overpriced. I'm convinced that it makes more sense to wait for the price correction to occur before buying any more shares.

To be clear, though, I'm not saying that the stock price will, in fact, hit a price level in the lower to mid-20s range. My model indicates that range is where the fair value lies. Still, I also cannot discount market sentiment keeping the stock above the ~$30 mark, given the company's positive momentum over the last few quarters.

Final Thoughts

The numbers tell a clear story: MPTI's steady but modest revenue and margin growth simply do not justify the current stock price. Also, the wild volatility-from a low of around $23.90 to nearly $70 and back to ~$46-only buttresses my position that speculation, not strong cash flow, is driving the market. Given this disconnect, I'd say hold off on buying the stock until the price better reflects the company's reality.

data.title: Berry Corporation: Increasing Focus On Utah Assets
data.description:
Berry Corporation (NASDAQ:BRY) successfully completed its previously announced financial transactions in late December 2024. These transactions pushed Berry's debt maturity out to December 2027, with the potential for extensions to December 2029.

Berry also has talked more about its preliminary plans for 2025, with 40% of its capex budget allocated towards its Utah Uinta Basin assets. Approximately 20% of Berry's total production comes from the Uinta Basin currently, but I can see that increasing by a few percent per year as Berry attempts to become less dependent on California and the uncertain regulatory environment there.

Berry's projected 2025 capex was a bit higher than I previously estimated, though, so I've trimmed its estimated value by $0.50 to a range of $5.00 to $6.00 per share. This also assumes high-$70 Brent oil after 2025. Berry likely needs that sort of oil price to start making more progress reducing its debt, although it should be able to make its minimum term loan repayments at $70 Brent.

Preliminary 2025 Plans

Berry included a slide in its December 2024 presentation that discussed its preliminary plans for 2025. It mentioned an estimated capex budget of $105 million to $120 million, split 60% to California and 40% to Utah.

Berry is planning on focusing on sidetracks in California (which it has several years of inventory ready) and is participating in a couple farm-in opportunities in Utah.

Berry is also looking to drill its own Utah wells with a JV partner to help offset some of the capex requirements. It mentioned that it was aiming for a JV announcement in February and wanted to start drilling its own Uinta Basin wells by the end of Q1 2025.

Addressing Dependence On California

Berry is likely to remain largely dependent on its California assets for now, although it is making some efforts to diversify. Around 81% of Berry's total production and 88% of its oil production came from California in Q3 2024.

Berry is expecting to devote around 60% of its 2025 capex to California and 40% to Utah, so this would shift its production slightly towards Utah from current levels. I'd still expect California to account for a high-70s percentage of Berry's total 2025 production and a mid-80s percentage of its 2025 oil production, though. I can see Berry's Utah production reaching a high-20s percentage of its total production within several years.

I'd also note that Berry may realize around $15 per barrel less for its Uinta Basin oil compared to its California oil, so the shift may incrementally increase Berry's oil differential by around $0.50 for 2025.

Berry is aiming for relatively flat production growth in 1H 2025 with low-single-digit growth in 2H 2025. With the Utah production having both a lower oil cut and a lower realized price (for oil) than its California production, low-single digits total production growth would lead to flat revenue growth, though.

Free Cash Flow Notes

Berry has an average of $289 million per year in expected cash requirements during 2025 to 2028. These are detailed in a table below (shown in millions of dollars).

This includes $45 million per year in required term loan repayments, estimated interest costs for its term loan (which should go down due to the required principal repayments), and $60 million per year in cash E&P and corporate G&A.

I've also assumed $20 million per year in asset retirement obligations, $112 million per year in maintenance (to hold value-weighted production flat), capex and $9 million per year in dividends at $0.03 per share per quarter.

Year Term Loan Repayments Interest G&A Extension Fees Dividends ARO Capex Total
2025 $45 $50 $60 $9 $20 $112 $296
2026 $45 $44 $60 $9 $20 $112 $290
2027 $45 $39 $60 $3 $9 $20 $112 $288
2028 $45 $33 $60 $3 $9 $20 $112 $282
2029 $270 $28 $60 $9 $20 $110 $497

Berry may be able to generate around $290 million EBITDA (excluding the impact of E&P Segment and Corporate G&A) at unhedged $70 Brent oil.

So at $70 Brent (and without any hedging proceeds), Berry would be able to cover its expected requirements including its current dividend, maintenance capex and required term loan repayments. It would have little to put towards further debt reduction or other purposes, though.

Berry's projected free cash flow would change by close to $40 million for every $5 change in Brent oil, so Berry could have around $80 million per year (from 2025 to 2028) in free cash flow above those expected requirements at unhedged $80 Brent.

Berry has the majority of its 2025 oil production hedged at approximately $76 Brent, but it has closer to one-third of its 2026 to 2028 oil production hedged around $80 Brent.

Notes On Valuation

I am trimming Berry's estimated value by $0.50 to a range of $5.00 to $6.00 per share now at long-term (after 2025) high-$70s Brent oil.

This reduction reflects slightly higher capex for Berry than I had previously modeled. I had previously assumed around $105 million in capex for Berry, while its December 2024 presentation mentions $105 million to $120 million in 2025 capex. Berry's January 2025 interview mentions a slightly different range of $105 million to $125 million for 2025 capex.

That amount of capex is expected to result in a slight amount of total production growth for Berry, although its value-weighted production may be unchanged. I've also assumed that a similar capex level would hold Berry's value-weighted production flat in future years as well.

Berry is in a challenging position where interest costs and corporate and E&P G&A costs add up to around over $10 per BOE, making it hard to improve its financial position significantly without improved oil prices (compared to strip) though. I believe that it can make some progress at high-$70s Brent, but oil prices would need to be higher than the low-$70s Brent strip for 2026.

Conclusion

Berry successfully completed various financial transactions and could push its term loan maturity out to December 2029 with small extension fee payments. It can cover its financial obligations at unhedged $70 Brent oil but wouldn't have much left over after items like interest costs, maintenance capex, and required term loan repayments, though.

At high-$70s Brent, it could make more progress in reducing its debt, generating an estimated $60 million in cash flow per year (before hedges) above what is needed to cover its current dividend and the required $45 million per year in term loan repayments. Berry's hedges would cut into that a bit, but I'd still estimate Berry's value at $5 to $6 in a long-term high-$70s Brent oil environment.

Berry's equity value is relatively marginal in a long-term $70 Brent environment, though, so there is significant risk involved with its stock.

data.title: PEO: Energy CEF With A 9% Yield, At A 10% Discount
data.description:
Introduction

The Adams Natural Resources Fund (NYSE:PEO) is an old closed-end fund started in 1929, with a distribution history going back 85+ years. It is a sister fund to one I've previously covered, the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX).

The fund has done well over its lifespan, returning significant amounts to shareholders via distributions. You can see this in the stark difference between PEO's price and total returns.

Note: Total returns include dividends paid, but not reinvested.

Also, the fund has a very long history, but YCharts is only able to take me back to January 31st, 1984.

Zooming in to the lifespan of a competing ETF, the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), we can see the difference that Adams Funds was able to make for investors. We can take this back to 1998 when XLE had its market debut.

Meanwhile, PEO was able to kick out a tremendous distribution rate during all those years. Its current rate is at 9.67%, with a high in the mid-double digits, and a low at around 5% over the last ten years. The average sits at just under 7%. Those distributions have been fairly level, however; remember that yield is a function of price. Investors actually holding through those ten years did not see much change to the distribution schedule, bar the lower years in 2021-2022, as shown below. That was a particularly challenging time for the oil and gas industry, to note.

Holdings

So what does PEO actually own? It's an equity fund, focused primarily on domestic energy firms, 89% of the fund is US-based. It invests in the energy sector broadly, but its holdings are concentrated in the integrated oil and gas, as well as the exploration and production subsectors.

This comes in the form of heavy investments in the top few holdings. Out of fifty-six holdings, the top ten comprise 65.3% of the fund's investment, and the top holding, Exxon Mobil Corp (XOM) makes up almost 25% of the fund's investments.

This kind of concentration is what can produce those kinds of excess returns over the market weight index we saw in the chart against XLE, but also poses a serious risk to investors that are worried about concentration risk. If something isolated to XOM were to take place and cause the stock to tank, it could very seriously affect PEO as it holds so much weight.

This risk is not necessarily one taken out of proportion with the stock market, as XLE still holds XOM at a 20%+ weighting, and Chevron (CVX) at a near-16% weight. In this way, PEO is actually underweight CVX compared to the index, and it's still the fund's second-largest holding.

The issue here is that Exxon Mobil is just so big compared to its peers that it's hard to have an energy fund without dedicating 20-25% to it.

Distributions

One of the major reasons investors would consider an investment in PEO is that it pays out that hefty dividend, as mentioned earlier. This has gone in a large range over its history, but today's 9.65% is fairly somewhat high.

Through the last year, the fund paid out $1.77 in dividends, with four quarterly distributions. These were primarily made up of investment income and long-term gains, with no return of capital or short-term gains reported for the year.

This is a good sign for many investors, who prefer to see the fund earning its dividends and not needing to return capital to shareholders to make up the promised payments. This kind of eating at the NAV of the fund to fuel returns is sometimes a bad sign for the fund, which may decline in price due to the losses. We don't see this with PEO.

Looking back ten years, we see that the fund only needed to return capital once, in 2018. That was a notably bad year for the fund return-wise, and for the energy sector more broadly.

Discount to NAV

Like most CEFs, PEO trades far from its net-asset value. The fund trades currently at a 10% discount, which is a bit above its 52-week average.

Here is a longer look at the discount history; the fund has effectively always traded at a discount over its lifespan, but the steepness of it varies dramatically. From a ten-year low of the mid-20s during the 2020 COVID-19 crash to a high of (7.53%) shortly after on the rebound in 2021.

The fund has been driven up substantially over the last year, and the discount gap has closed. This poses a risk to investors, who could suffer losses unrelated to the underlying assets' performances, if the discount steepens out back toward its 10-year average of (14.64%).

I consider this the largest red flag currently with PEO, as it's unclear if this level of discount will persist or change in the near or medium-term futures. Finding the right entry point is imperative for investors worried about this factor.

It should be of note that investors have the option of taking their distributions in shares instead of cash, which could be used to help mitigate the risk of picking purchase dates for the fund. However, that is not an option for all investors, some of whom need the income generated by their investments to pay for their lifestyles.

Suitability

PEO is a fund that is suitable for income investors broadly, but in certain doses. I believe that it could serve as a great way to enhance yield and exposure to energy in an equity portfolio, as well as generate income as part of an income portfolio.

For all investors, I do not recommend putting more than 5% of a portfolio into any single closed-end fund, as the discount/premium aspect of them could make an otherwise lucrative investment illiquid at a moment's notice. This poses a risk for rebalancing and future investment opportunities, and so I suggest spreading investments out across CEFs to mitigate this risk.

To that end, I am recommending that aggressive investors consider up to a 5% allocation to PEO in their portfolios. Moderate investors could consider up to a 3% allocation, and conservative investors a 1% allocation to PEO.

As with any equity fund, there are risks associated with the stock market as well, on top of those I've already covered, such as the concentration risk with Exxon and the discount risk.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I am bullish on the Adams Natural Resources Fund and I am giving it a buy rating. Its high distribution and outperformance against the index is notable, and its history shows that this isn't a one-off instance. Investors do need to note the narrowing discount poses a risk, especially if a steeper one opens up in the future. However, we don't know the future. This may be a new normal for PEO.

One of the selling points for many income investors is that regardless of the outcome of the discount issue, the fund will continue its regular quarterly distributions, and carries a forward yield of over 9.5%.

I am considering adding PEO to my CEF portfolio, and it is definitely on my watch list.

Thanks for reading.
